title: French Omelette

subs.name: silken tofu (100 g) blended smooth — a vegan variation with a different texture
subs.notes: cook on medium heat rather than high

subs.name: ghee — gives a similar richness with a faintly nutty aroma
spring-onion greens or fresh coriander leaves

steps.text: Crack the eggs into a bowl and whisk vigorously with a pinch of salt until the whites and yolks are fully combined — the mixture should be slightly foamy. Do not add milk or water; the French omelette relies solely on egg for its silky texture.

Set a 20 cm non-stick frying pan over high heat. Add the butter and swirl until it melts and the foam subsides — the butter should be hot but not yet browning. If it turns golden, reduce the heat slightly for the next attempt.

Pour in all the beaten egg at once. Immediately begin shaking the pan back and forth while stirring with a heatproof spatula in small rapid circles. The egg will cook in 30–45 seconds and should look softly scrambled and glossy, not dry.

The moment the egg is just set but still glistens on top, stop stirring. Tilt the pan away from you and use the spatula to fold one third of the omelette over the centre, then tip the pan further so the omelette rolls onto the plate seam-side down.

The finished omelette should be a pale ivory-gold on the outside and custardy within, shaped like a small log. Season with a grind of pepper and scatter parsley on top. Serve immediately — a French omelette does not wait.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: butter → ghee — gives a similar richness with a faintly nutty aroma (use the same quantity); egg → silken tofu (100 g) blended smooth — a vegan variation with a different texture (cook on medium heat rather than high); parsley → spring-onion greens or fresh coriander leaves (both work as garnish)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A good-quality non-stick tawa (24–26 cm) works perfectly in place of the traditional omelette pan. Indian gas burners run hotter than European electric hobs, so keep the flame medium-high rather than full blast and be ready to move fast. Amul unsalted butter performs exactly as needed here.
